metadata.title: Environmentalism among poor and rich countries: using natural language processing to handle perfunctory support and rising powers

abstract: In international politics, is environmental protection largely a "rich-country" priority? We perceive four reasons why, although individual exceptions are possible, the answer would be yes: as a country meets more of its basic economic needs, it can better take on environmental policy's long-term thinking, policy expenses, collective action problems, and quality-of-life issues. To cut through lip service paid by governments that are not serious about environmental protection, and the fact that the "BASIC" countries (Brazil, South Africa, India, China) occupy a gray area between rich and poor, we employ computer-assisted textual analyses on all 3,774 paragraphs of statements made by national governments between 1995-2012 in the Committee on Trade and Environment (CTE) within the World Trade Organization (WTO). Controlling for other factors, we find a general pattern of environmental discussions increasing as development level increases. This contributes substantively and methodologically to the literatures on the environment/development nexus and rising powers.

paragraphs: In international politics, is environmental protection largely a "rich-country" priority? That is, when nation-states are at lower development levels, do they tend to be less concerned about protecting the natural environment?

Although some researchers argue that the answer is no (e.g. Guha, 2000;Martinez-Alier, 1995;Mitchell, 2010), we perceive four reasons why the answer would be yes. With their greater material resources, richer states are better positioned than poorer states to: (1) forego short-term economic gains to get long-term environmental benefits; (2) afford the actual costs and opportunity costs of environmental stewardship; (3) assume the role of policy entrepreneurs who overcome collective action problems in contributing to environmental public goods; and (4) host a robust, enfranchised middle class that makes demands about environmental protection and other quality-of-life issues. Individual exceptions-such as a poor island nation facing an existential threat from climate change-certainly are possible. But in general, it is easier for states to take on environmental policy's longterm thinking, policy expenses, collective action problems, and quality-of-life issues as a country meets more of its basic economic needs.

Unfortunately, researching the nature of links between development level and environmentalism is complicated. For one thing, environmental protection is like human rights, inequality, and other sensitive issues in international relations: governments that would prefer not to talk about the issue at all, or to take any action, are wary about explicitly opposing it. Therefore in international environmental forums, governments that do not care much about environmental protection nevertheless participate in negotiations and pay lip service to that policy area (Bernstein, 2001).

The situation is further complicated by the "BASIC" states of Brazil, Afrique du Sud (South Africa), India, and China. In the past two decades these states experienced substantial economic growth, and in 2009 they formally banded under the BASIC nomenclature to shape the outcome of the Copenhagen climate negotiations (Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012;Zhu, 2013). With improved capabilities and visibility (Copelovitch & Ohls, 2012;Hurrell & Narlikar, 2006;Tierney, 2014), these dynamic countries continue to hold high-level meetings to coordinate their environment-related negotiating positions. Yet with their middling development levels that blur traditional distinctions between the "rich North" and "poor South" (Voeten, 2000), it can be hard to anticipate whether the BASIC states would prioritize environmental considerations over other concerns (Hurrell & Sengupta, 2012;Johnson & Urpelainen, 2020).

To cut through these real-world challenges, we use a natural language processing (NLP) approach to examine governments' discussions of environmental protection when an economic objective-trade-is also at stake. We employ computer-assisted textual analyses on all 3,774 paragraphs of statements made by national governments between 1995-2012 in negotiations at the Committee on Trade and Environment (CTE) within the World Trade Organization (WTO). 1 This is an ideal context because it is open to a wide variety of countries, operates by consensus (so each government has an incentive to talk), and permits governments to raise their environmental priorities, economic priorities, or combinations of the two (McKibben, 2016). We first analyze participants collectively. Then, we analyze the BASIC countries specifically in order to investigate how much the BASIC states' environmental dialogue differs from other states, from one other, and across time as their development levels vary.

After controlling for regime type, year, and other confounders, our analyses find a general pattern of environmental discussions increasing as development level increases. Environmental discussions can even be spotted varying for an individual country as its development level changes over time. A notable example is Brazil, whose per capita Gross Domestic Product (GDP) has both increased and decreased in the 1995-2012 period. Within the CTE negotiations, at higher levels of per capita GDP Brazil talks in a more "environmental" way: less about market access, and more about sustainable development. At lower levels of per capita GDP, the situation is reversed.

By showing that environmental discussions are more strongly associated with richer countries, we weigh in on long-running debates about the environment/ development nexus (e.g. Barkin, 2003;Davey, 2009;Franzen & Vogl, 2013a;Guha, 2000;Inglehart, 2008;Jinnah, 2017;Johnson & Urpelainen, 2020;Martinez-Alier, 2014;Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012;Mitchell, 2010;Najam, 2005;Neumayer, 2001;Retallack, 2000;Stern, 2017). And, by revealing a potential tipping point at which the BASIC countries may embrace environmentalism more tightly, we also contribute to the growing literature on rising powers (e.g. Donno & Rudra, 2014;Hallding et al., 2013;Hopewell, 2015;Tierney, 2014;Weinhardt & ten Brink 2020). Beyond these substantive contributions to international political economy, we also make methodological contributions: (a) demonstrating how researchers can handle the lip service that governments pay to sensitive policy areas; (b) showing how to sidestep the pitfalls of institutional mandates; and (c) unpacking how computerassisted textual analysis blends positivism and interpretivism, social science and computer science, quantitative and qualitative data (Nielsen, 2017). All three contributions travel well beyond the subfield of international relations.

sections.header_raw: Conceptual framework: development level and environmentalism
sections.header_norm: conceptual framework development level and environmentalism
paragraphs: Since the rise of international environmental policy in the late twentieth century, scholars and practitioners have debated whether a state's development level predicts how much it cares about environmental protection.

A long-standing debate Some researchers argue that there is no correlation. In the early post-Cold War period, many poorer countries perceived environmental protection as a rich-country concern that had been foisted on them in international forums (Narlikar, 2003). Over time, however, environmental protection presented sub-issues and trade-offs that could confound stark North/South generalizations (Bernauer & Nguyen, 2015;Dunlap & York, 2008;Hurrell & Sengupta, 2012;Kolcava et al., 2019;Mitchell, 1998Mitchell, , 2010)).

Climate change is often cited as proof. In international climate talks, particular poor states (e.g. island nations) have called for much greater climate action, while particular rich states (e.g. fossil fuel exporters) have dragged their feet (B€ ackstrand & Elgstr€ om, 2013;Jinnah, 2017;Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012). 2 In this view, environmentalism is predicted not by development level, but by idiosyncratic country characteristics such as geography, key economic sectors, or natural resource endowments.

Yet other researchers go further, positing that development level and environmentalism are not uncorrelated-instead, they are correlated negatively. For instance, the literature on the "Environmentalism of the Poor" (EOP) argues that poor individuals (and by extension, the Global South) would be more highly motivated as stewards of the natural environment (Guha, 2000). One reason is their proximity to nature: "Whereas wealthier people of the North have in general lost the idea of the environment as their source of livelihood, the poor and largely rural populations of the South are more connected to the environment, and thus have a more intimate understanding of what is at stake by not managing it carefully" (Martinez-Alier, 2014, 240).

In addition, the poor's marginalized status in global markets makes them more reliant on local economies, where activities that harm the local environment are relatively easy for community members to spot and discipline (Retallack, 2000). Furthermore, environmental prescriptions such as maintaining a small ecological footprint or a modest level of consumerism might be easier to implement in poorer countries, where per capita consumption is already much lower than in richer countries (Davey, 2009, 8). Finally, because poorer countries are especially vulnerable to environmental degradation (Brechin & Kempton, 1994;Franzen & Vogl, 2013b) and many are led by authoritarian regimes (Battig & Bernauer, 2009), governments in the Global South may be both more animated and more forceful than richer democracies in implementing environmental protection.

Development level and environmentalism: reasons for a positive association However, we perceive four key reasons to think that development level and environmentalism would be correlated positively, after accounting for regime type and other country characteristics. First, environmentalism entails thinking about the long term. Environmental stewardship pays off over decades or centuries-and even then, the benefits may be abstract (Neumayer, 2001). For poorer states, environmental exploitation promises material gains that are more tangible, immediate, and difficult to resist.

Second, environmental stewardship is expensive. Its expenses lie not only in the human and monetary resources that would need to be spent, but also in the opportunity costs of natural resource exploitation that would need to be foregone. In poorer countries, where fundamental concerns such as food or employment loom large, it is especially difficult to justify the money, manpower, and opportunity costs needed for environmental protection (Najam, 2005).

Third, many environmental issues suffer from collective action problems (Johnson & Urpelainen, 2012). A group of governments may aim for some collective level of contributions toward an international public good (e.g. clean air)-but because contributions are difficult to enforce and the public good is difficult to exclude from non-contributors, each government also faces temptations to free-ride (Barkin, 2003). While richer states may have the wherewithal to multiply their own contributions to compensate for numerous free-riders, poorer states are unlikely to be able to serve as such environmental policy entrepreneurs.

Fourth, environmentalism requires political will. While the previous three reasons focus on governments themselves, this final reason involves the link between governments and their citizenry. Demands for environmental protection and other quality-of-life issues often escalate as the size of a country's middle class grows (Prakash 2002), 3 but many developing countries still lack a robust and enfranchised middle class. Without an influential portion of citizens pushing for environmentalism internally, governments have leeway to prioritize other policy areas (Bechtel et al., 2012;Inglehart, 2008). Moreover, even the EOP literature points out a historical reason for governments in the Global South to de-prioritize environmentalism: their delayed industrialization also slowed their collision course with pollution, resource depletion, and other "effluents of affluence" (Martinez-Alier, 1995, 9).

Notice that three of the reasons above-long-term thinking, policy expenses, and overcoming collective action problems-are about the removal of impediments that would deter governments from prioritizing the natural environment. Meanwhile, the fourth reason-a robust middle class that calls for environmental protection and other quality-of-life demands-is what can push a government to protect the environment even if it is not already inclined to do so. Even among researchers who highlight "the environmentalism of the poor," there is ready acknowledgment that poor people and poor countries may need to de-prioritize environmental protection not because they lack the will, but because they lack the capacity (Davey, 2009, 8).

The four reasons are not always absent in poorer countries. However, in richer countries, more of the reasons are likely to be present simultaneously. And if such a confluence is more likely in countries at higher development levels, that leads to an observable implication: in general, environmental protection would be prioritized more by richer countries than by poorer ones. There may be individual exceptions-related to, for example, what else is happening in the world at a particular time, or what a country's regime type is, or what natural resource endowment a country has. But, after controlling for such confounders, the pattern would be a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism.

This pattern shares some intuition with the economics literature on an "Environmental Kuznets Curve" (EKC), but our approach also contains important differences. Writing in the mid-twentieth century, the economist Simon Kuznets conjectured that graphing a country's GDP on a horizontal axis and a country's internal economic inequality on a vertical axis would produce an upside-down U shape: as a country got richer, its internal inequality might initially increase, but eventually reach a peak and steadily decline. Recognizing that the main way for a country to get richer in the twentieth century was by moving its economy from agriculture to industry and eventually to services, subsequent economists conjectured a similarly concave graph related to environmental quality. Pollution might increase as a country transitioned from agriculture to industry, but then pollution might decline as a country transitioned from industry to services. Since many economic studies have focused on a single country or a single pollutant, the empirical record is mixed, and economists continue to debate the existence of an Environmental Kuznets Curve (Carson, 2010;Cole et al., 1997;Grossman & Krueger, 1991;Selden & Song, 1994;Stern, 2017).

Although our conceptual framework shares the view that a country's development level is a key explanatory variable, we diverge from the EKC literature in several key respects. For one thing, we include economists' insights and also transcend them, recognizing that "development" is not only economic but also social, anthropological, and political. Moreover, our outcome of interest is not a particular pollutant, but environmentalism more broadly. In addition, instead of being tailored to a specific country or year, our anticipated pattern pertains to a swath of countries and years. Furthermore, due to the political realities explored below, we will look for a positive relationship between development level and environmentalism in a novel context, and we will investigate many different countries that are simultaneously considering a variety of environmental and economic topics.

In international climate talks, particular poor states (e.g. island nations) have called for much greater climate action, while particular rich states (e.g. fossil fuel exporters) have dragged their feet (B€ ackstrand & Elgstr€ om, 2013;Jinnah, 2017;Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012). 2 In this view, environmentalism is predicted not by development level, but by idiosyncratic country characteristics such as geography, key economic sectors, or natural resource endowments.

Yet other researchers go further, positing that development level and environmentalism are not uncorrelated-instead, they are correlated negatively. For instance, the literature on the "Environmentalism of the Poor" (EOP) argues that poor individuals (and by extension, the Global South) would be more highly motivated as stewards of the natural environment (Guha, 2000). One reason is their proximity to nature: "Whereas wealthier people of the North have in general lost the idea of the environment as their source of livelihood, the poor and largely rural populations of the South are more connected to the environment, and thus have a more intimate understanding of what is at stake by not managing it carefully" (Martinez-Alier, 2014, 240).

In addition, the poor's marginalized status in global markets makes them more reliant on local economies, where activities that harm the local environment are relatively easy for community members to spot and discipline (Retallack, 2000). Furthermore, environmental prescriptions such as maintaining a small ecological footprint or a modest level of consumerism might be easier to implement in poorer countries, where per capita consumption is already much lower than in richer countries (Davey, 2009, 8). Finally, because poorer countries are especially vulnerable to environmental degradation (Brechin & Kempton, 1994;Franzen & Vogl, 2013b) and many are led by authoritarian regimes (Battig & Bernauer, 2009), governments in the Global South may be both more animated and more forceful than richer democracies in implementing environmental protection.

Development level and environmentalism: reasons for a positive association However, we perceive four key reasons to think that development level and environmentalism would be correlated positively, after accounting for regime type and other country characteristics. First, environmentalism entails thinking about the long term. Environmental stewardship pays off over decades or centuries-and even then, the benefits may be abstract (Neumayer, 2001). For poorer states, environmental exploitation promises material gains that are more tangible, immediate, and difficult to resist.

Second, environmental stewardship is expensive. Its expenses lie not only in the human and monetary resources that would need to be spent, but also in the opportunity costs of natural resource exploitation that would need to be foregone. In poorer countries, where fundamental concerns such as food or employment loom large, it is especially difficult to justify the money, manpower, and opportunity costs needed for environmental protection (Najam, 2005).

Third, many environmental issues suffer from collective action problems (Johnson & Urpelainen, 2012). A group of governments may aim for some collective level of contributions toward an international public good (e.g. clean air)-but because contributions are difficult to enforce and the public good is difficult to exclude from non-contributors, each government also faces temptations to free-ride (Barkin, 2003). While richer states may have the wherewithal to multiply their own contributions to compensate for numerous free-riders, poorer states are unlikely to be able to serve as such environmental policy entrepreneurs.

Fourth, environmentalism requires political will. While the previous three reasons focus on governments themselves, this final reason involves the link between governments and their citizenry. Demands for environmental protection and other quality-of-life issues often escalate as the size of a country's middle class grows (Prakash 2002), 3 but many developing countries still lack a robust and enfranchised middle class. Without an influential portion of citizens pushing for environmentalism internally, governments have leeway to prioritize other policy areas (Bechtel et al., 2012;Inglehart, 2008). Moreover, even the EOP literature points out a historical reason for governments in the Global South to de-prioritize environmentalism: their delayed industrialization also slowed their collision course with pollution, resource depletion, and other "effluents of affluence" (Martinez-Alier, 1995, 9).

Notice that three of the reasons above-long-term thinking, policy expenses, and overcoming collective action problems-are about the removal of impediments that would deter governments from prioritizing the natural environment. Meanwhile, the fourth reason-a robust middle class that calls for environmental protection and other quality-of-life demands-is what can push a government to protect the environment even if it is not already inclined to do so. Even among researchers who highlight "the environmentalism of the poor," there is ready acknowledgment that poor people and poor countries may need to de-prioritize environmental protection not because they lack the will, but because they lack the capacity (Davey, 2009, 8).

The four reasons are not always absent in poorer countries. However, in richer countries, more of the reasons are likely to be present simultaneously. And if such a confluence is more likely in countries at higher development levels, that leads to an observable implication: in general, environmental protection would be prioritized more by richer countries than by poorer ones. There may be individual exceptions-related to, for example, what else is happening in the world at a particular time, or what a country's regime type is, or what natural resource endowment a country has. But, after controlling for such confounders, the pattern would be a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism.

This pattern shares some intuition with the economics literature on an "Environmental Kuznets Curve" (EKC), but our approach also contains important differences. Writing in the mid-twentieth century, the economist Simon Kuznets conjectured that graphing a country's GDP on a horizontal axis and a country's internal economic inequality on a vertical axis would produce an upside-down U shape: as a country got richer, its internal inequality might initially increase, but eventually reach a peak and steadily decline. Recognizing that the main way for a country to get richer in the twentieth century was by moving its economy from agriculture to industry and eventually to services, subsequent economists conjectured a similarly concave graph related to environmental quality. Pollution might increase as a country transitioned from agriculture to industry, but then pollution might decline as a country transitioned from industry to services. Since many economic studies have focused on a single country or a single pollutant, the empirical record is mixed, and economists continue to debate the existence of an Environmental Kuznets Curve (Carson, 2010;Cole et al., 1997;Grossman & Krueger, 1991;Selden & Song, 1994;Stern, 2017).

Although our conceptual framework shares the view that a country's development level is a key explanatory variable, we diverge from the EKC literature in several key respects. For one thing, we include economists' insights and also transcend them, recognizing that "development" is not only economic but also social, anthropological, and political. Moreover, our outcome of interest is not a particular pollutant, but environmentalism more broadly. In addition, instead of being tailored to a specific country or year, our anticipated pattern pertains to a swath of countries and years. Furthermore, due to the political realities explored below, we will look for a positive relationship between development level and environmentalism in a novel context, and we will investigate many different countries that are simultaneously considering a variety of environmental and economic topics.

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
sections.header_raw: Two complications: perfunctory support and rising powers
sections.header_norm: two complications perfunctory support and rising powers
paragraphs: The expected pattern is that, in general, environmental protection would be prioritized more by richer countries than by poorer ones. However, testing this is complicated by political realities. For one thing, some countries-in particular, the environmental negotiating group of the "BASIC" states of Brazil, South Africa, India, and China-do not neatly fit into a rich/poor dichotomy. The remarkable economic growth of the BASIC states in recent decades has set them apart from countries in the "poor South," but without fully catapulting them into the ranks of the "rich North" (Donno & Rudra, 2014;Hallding et al., 2013;Hopewell, 2015;Zhu, 2013). Given their different regime types and development levels, the four states certainly are not identical to each other, but they nevertheless self-identify as a cohort. They adopted the BASIC nomenclature in 2009 when they formally coordinated their negotiating positions for the Copenhagen climate talks, and since then they have used high-level meetings to coordinate their negotiating positions in additional areas of environmental policy (Jinnah, 2017;Zhu, 2013). With their middling and dynamic levels of development, the BASIC states' policy priorities might be in flux.

Another complication is that, especially in strictly environmental forums such as climate change talks, governments that are ambivalent about environmentalism face incentives to hide this. Environmental protection is a sensitive issue that, like human rights or inequality, harnesses deep devotion from some governments and unconcern from others. Yet for the unconcerned, little good would come from stating that explicitly or opting out of all international forums that deal with the issue (Bernstein, 2001). Such behavior would stand out, inviting pushback from a variety of actors. Indeed, research has shown that even ordinary people succumb to pressure to pay lip service to environmentalism: an in-depth analysis of several international public opinion polls found that the strong correlation between wealth and environmentalism was obscured when poorer respondents "acquiesced" to environmentalism that they did not truly embrace (Franzen & Vogl, 2013a).

Although that research examined individuals, a similar dynamic likely faces countries in international negotiations. Governments that do not care much about environmental protection nevertheless face pressures to pay lip service to environmentalism (Johnson, 2016). Opting out of all environmental forums is not a viable option. Instead, a savvier and subtler move is to participate, but deflect. For example, they can raise administrative points or call for more research. If governments that do not prioritize environmental protection were operating in an international forum that discusses environmental policy alongside other policy areas, they also could try to shift the conversation to policy areas that they do prioritize (McKibben, 2016). Since there is pressure for governments to make perfunctory statements of support even if they are not serious about environmental stewardship, researchers who want to understand where priorities lie will need to pay attention to the topics that governments raise-not just the motivations they claim or the sentiments they express (Krebs & Jackson, 2007).

Thus, this research is complicated by the lip service that governments may pay to environmental protection and the fact that the BASIC countries confound simple rich/poor dichotomies. To cut through governments' lip service to environmentalism, we examine governments' discussions of environmental protection when an economic objective-trade-is also at stake. This permits us to spot instances when governments circumvent discussion of environmentalism by raising administrative points, calling for more research, or shifting the conversation to another policy area. To sidestep crude and limiting dichotomies of rich and poor, we operationalize development level as a continuous variable, then scrutinize governments as a whole and also the BASIC states separately. Since development levels vary both across and within the BASIC states, we can compare Brazil, South Africa, India, and China to each other, and also to themselves at different development levels.

This points to two specific research questions. First, when governments are free to talk about environmental protection alongside other issues, are the topics discussed by richer states more environmental than those discussed by poorer ones? Second, as Brazil's, South Africa's, India's, and China's development levels have increased (or decreased), have they talked in a more (or less) environmental way?
Research approach: natural language processing (NLP)

To answer these questions, we use a natural language processing (NLP) approach. We undertake computer-assisted textual analyses of the entire population of statements made by national governments between 1995-2012 in the CTE, within the World Trade Organization. Compared to interviews or surveys, these negotiating transcripts are better suited to our purposes: rather than being responses to researchers, these naturally occurring texts are direct products of conversations among governments (Bail, 2014, 467). And since those conversations occur repeatedly, they yield useful longitudinal data.

sections.header_raw: The WTO's committee on trade and environment (CTE)
sections.header_norm: the wto s committee on trade and environment cte
paragraphs: The CTE, launched concurrently with the WTO in 1995, is an international forum for discussing environmental and economic concerns. 4 Its 10-point mandate covers (1) trade rules and environmental agreements; (2) environmental protection and the trading system; (3) taxes and other environmental requirements; (4) transparency of environmental trade measures; (5) disputes; (6) environment and trade liberalization; (7) domestically prohibited goods; (8) intellectual property; (9) services; and 10) the WTO's relations with inter-governmental and non-governmental organizations. 5 The medium scope of the CTE is useful for our research questions: potential topics are more bounded than in a catch-all forum such as the UN General Assembly, but also less constrained than in a strictly environmental forum such as the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change.

All governments in the WTO's nearly global membership can participate in the CTE (Johnson, 2015). That means countries at very different development levels interact. Such cross-sectional variation exists alongside cross-temporal variation, since individual countries' development levels-particularly for Brazil, South Africa, India, and China-have changed markedly since 1995. 6 Like many international forums, WTO bodies use a one-country-one-vote system, with major decisions requiring consensus. 7 Consequently, governments at all development levels "matter" in the CTE and have incentives to participate in discussions. Governments' representatives include not only trade officials, but also officials with environmental or technical expertise. The CTE can issue non-binding recommendations, but explicit votes on binding policies are reserved for separate WTO meetings.

For our purposes, being one step removed from binding policies is advantageous. This is a diplomatic context, and governments' statements may be instrumental. But in the CTE their statements are unlikely to be outright lies-indeed, previous research finds a robust positive correlation between the stringency of a government's domestic environmental policies (Hsu, 2016) and its discussion of environmentalism within the CTE (Johnson & Urpelainen, 2020). There is less incentive to lie since governments choose when to talk, and they can try to steer the conversation to what they want to talk about. For example, if the discussion turns to climate change, and that is not a key concern of a particular government, the government can offer a bland acknowledgment of another party's concern but then deflect by calling for more research, raising administrative points, or shifting the subject to something that is a key concern.

Hence, the CTE is ideal for our investigation. It permits governments to discuss their environmental priorities, economic priorities, or both. It also delivers longitudinal textual data about countries at very different development levels. Meanwhile the WTO's one-country-one-vote system, which is common to many other international forums, incentivizes a wide variety of governments to share their views. Finally, because voting happens in separate WTO meetings, the stakes in the CTE remain high enough to discourage unfettered "cheap talk," yet there is less temptation for governments to deliver lengthy lies about their priorities in CTE meetings in order to manipulate voting outcomes (Krebs & Jackson, 2007).

paragraphs: The CTE meets regularly, two or three times per year. That means many opportunities-across countries, across time-to examine what is said. Importantly, the regularized schedule ensures that meetings showcase governments' interactions but are not driven by those interactions. For instance, the group does not convene only in times of conflict. For 1995-2012 there are nearly 60 meeting minutes, each containing scores of statements.

The WTO Secretariat makes CTE meeting minutes publicly available on the WTO website, in English, in a standardized format. The minutes are highly detailed: starting with the first meeting, all statements are not only transcribed but also attributed to the governments making them. Many statements are one paragraph long.

A paragraph is the unit of analysis. The minutes already contain paragraph breaks. Primarily, breaks distinguish different speakers; secondarily, they signal different strains of thought when a single government makes a longer statement. 8 The overall population of utterances by individual national governments between 1995-2012 is 3,774 paragraphs. 9 If this textual data were monopolized by richer countries, then the key explanatory variable (per capita GDP) would be truncated, with the substantive result that the data would not give much insight into poorer countries. But three probes reassure that this does not apply:
1. The top CTE speakers are a mix of countries from the Global South (e.g.

Argentina, Brazil, India) and the Global North (e.g. Japan, Norway, the United States). The relatively wealthy countries that comprise the European Community/Union are represented by the European Commission, which also is a top speaker. 2. The 3,774 statements are contributed fairly evenly by the Global South and the Global North. Classifying countries into a richer North and a poorer South using the per capita income that the World Bank considers the dividing line between developed and developing countries, 10 about 60 percent of the CTE statements are from the Global North and about 40 percent are from the Global South. 3. Even the world's poorest countries are active participants in the CTE.

Employing the World Banks' three-tier classification of developing countries, 11 it becomes clear that of the CTE statements from the Global South, lowincome countries account for about 25 percent, lower middle-income countries account for about 25 percent, and upper middle-income countries account for slightly less than 50 percent.

In short, some governments make more statements than others, reflecting the differences in power and discourse control that are well known in domestic and international politics (Davis & Bermeo, 2009;Panke, 2017). Yet nearly all WTO members speak in the CTE, and the data covers countries at a wide range of development levels.

paragraphs: Using the steps detailed in Part IV.A of the Appendix, we ready the 3,774 paragraphs for computer-assisted textual analysis. This yields a "word-document matrix" in which each document (here, a paragraph) is reduced to a single vector containing the frequency count of each word the document contains (Lucas et al., 2015). That is, the columns list all the documents, the rows list all the words, and the cells contain the frequencies with which each word appears in each document. This matrix enables comparisons of documents (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013).

sections.header_raw: The importance of topics and proportions
sections.header_norm: the importance of topics and proportions
paragraphs: As elaborated in Part II.B of the Appendix, the analyses will focus on topics and their proportions-not sentiment. Sentiment is difficult to pinpoint in diplomatic contexts, where participants rarely see much benefit in taking overt stands "against" something. As with human rights and other sensitive issues in international relations, governments may perfunctorily profess support for environmental stewardship even if they are not serious about it. For example, consider this CTE statement by Saudi Arabia, an oil-endowed country definitely not known for embracing environmentalism:

The representative of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia supported international efforts to combat climate change. Climate change issues should be dealt with by the UNFCCC, and not in the WTO, as their organization had the mandate and relevant expertise. Climate change was an environmental, not a trade, issue. The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia recalled that environmental issues had been included in the [WTO's Doha Development Agenda] in a very limited manner. Therefore, the CTE should not disregard the clear confines of the Ministerial mandate by engaging in a broad-ranging discussion on climate change. 12 Note how Saudi Arabia opens with a short and vague expression in support of environmental protection-then pivots to a longer and more specific list of administrative points, hoping to prevent other governments from discussing climate change in the CTE at all. The first sentence's supportive sentiment is not only questionable, but also very different from the administrative objections that constitute most of the statement.

Raising administrative points or calling for more research are not the only ways in which governments that do not prioritize environmental protection can participate but deflect. In a forum such as the CTE, where environmental protection is considered alongside another policy area, governments have an additional option: they can devote a greater portion of their statements to topics that they do prioritize. For instance, Part II.B of the Appendix contrasts two CTE statements by Benin and the European Community (EC). Whereas most of the EC's statement details its aims in combating climate change, Benin briefly notes the general need to protect the environment and then devotes most of its statement to discussing its economic needs for market access and technical assistance. The areas of emphasis are very different.

The Saudi Arabia, Benin, and EC examples underscore the usefulness of examining a forum in which environmental protection is discussed alongside a different policy area. Such a forum permits a close look at which topics governments choose to cover, and in what proportions. In doing so, we demonstrate how researchers can move beyond governments' perfunctory support for environmentalism, instead uncovering deeper patterns in governmental deflections and emphases.

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
paragraphs: Our research questions-focusing on topics, rather than sentiment or ideology-are ideal for topic modeling. As explained in greater detail in Part III of the Appendix, topic modeling analyzes the frequencies and co-occurrences of words in each document, then assigns probabilities that any given document contains any generated topic. This uncovers underlying topics, their connectedness, and their variation across actors or time (Blei et al., 2003).

We use a probabilistic topic model that learns-not assumes-the vocabulary of the tokenized texts, discovering their hidden thematic structure (Lucas et al., 2015, 261). 13 Specifically, we employ the Structural Topic Model (STM), a modified probabilistic topic model that permits a single document to contain multiple topics. Like other probabilistic topic models, the STM utilizes probability distributions and observable word frequencies to make inferences about unobservable latent topics that give rise to observed word use. The input is the aforementioned word-document matrix. The output is a set of estimated probabilities that each document is composed of each topic (Blei et al., 2003).

Since the probabilities sum to 1 for each document, the probabilities are like estimates of the proportion of each document that is composed of each topic. This is ideal for parsing sensitive issues such as environmental protection, where speakers may quickly mention one topic but then emphasize a different topic. It is also well suited to the WTO's CTE, where governments discuss their economic priorities, environmental priorities, or both. For instance, if a government is talking about market access for environmental services, then the proportions portray that mix of both (rather than coding the statement as just one or the other).

Ceding initial pattern-recognition to the computer in this way protects researchers from their own preconceptions and cognitive limitations. In fact, the computer's quantification of the texts enables something that is almost impossible for humans. That is, it not only recognizes that an observation might not fit neatly into a single topic, but also identifies how many topics it contains, and in what proportions.

Among probabilistic topic models, the STM is a particularly attractive modification. For one thing, it provides fast, transparent, replicable analyses without requiring many a priori assumptions about the texts (Tingley, 2017). Human researchers do not need to anticipate every topic that might be observed, and there is no assumption that topics are always independent of one another. Furthermore, the STM permits covariates to "structure" the topic model's prior distributions for document-topic proportions and topic-word proportions (Roberts et al., 2014). In other words, through standard regression models the STM can incorporate metadata: covariates depicting document characteristics such as speaker, sequencing, and date (Lucas et al., 2015, 255).

paragraphs: The key explanatory variable to be tested is a speaker's development level. We capture it with per capita GDP. 14 This continuous variable varies across countries and time, accounts for population, and avoids presumptive dividing lines between rich and poor countries.

We also include several control variables. First, since CTE discussions are likely to reflect the crises, negotiations, or breakthroughs occurring in international affairs in any given year, we employ year fixed-effects. Second, since initiating a topic early in a meeting sets an agenda or focal point to which later statements are likely to respond, we control for the order in which statements were made. Third, since authoritarian governments are expected to speak for and be responsive to a narrower swath of their citizenry compared to democratic governments, we include speakers' Polity IV scores (which range from À10 for strongly authoritarian regimes to þ10 for strongly democratic regimes). And fourth, since distinctive characteristics of a particular country-such as its geography, key economic sectors, or natural resource endowments-may shape priorities regardless of development level (David, 1991), we use country indicator variables. 15

paragraphs: But before undertaking regressions, topics themselves need to be identified-and for this, the STM requires human researchers to direct the number of topics, k, the computer will seek. 16 The number must be neither too low nor too high (DiMaggio, 2015). Determining an appropriate number involves both statistical and substantive validation (Wilkerson & Casas, 2017)-see Part IV.C of the Appendix for more details.

The CTE's 10-point mandate suggests that an appropriate number of topics is at least 10, so we iteratively set k to all numbers from 10 to 100, then employ goodness-of-fit statistics (Grimmer, 2010). There is no single "right" statistic. Therefore we look holistically at four-held-out likelihood, residuals, semantic coherence, and lower bound-and home in on k-values that do well on multiple measures simultaneously (Roberts et al. 2019).

As shown in Figures A4 and A5 of the Appendix, an appropriate k immediately appears to be between 20 and 40, and can be further pinpointed to the low 30 s, where the four measures approximately plateau. With the range narrowed statistically, we check various k-values in the low 30 s substantively: we personally deliberate the top five stemmed words and the words that are frequent and exclusive to each identified topic (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). The aim is to uncover a number k that produces useful, meaningful, and semantically coherent sets of words. Ultimately we choose k ¼ 32, a strong performer statistically and substantively.

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
paragraphs: With k set, the computer finds 32 distinct topics and identifies the top stemmed words and the top frequent/exclusive words for each. The human researcher then determines labels, as detailed in Part IV.D of the Appendix. This process involves reading underlying texts that the computer identified as exemplar documents and understanding how the words from the frequency lists are actually used in context, across multiple documents (Lucas et al., 2015, 264). It also involves validating the labels by examining whether topic proportions align with expectations about international negotiations (e.g. the expectation that climate change became a more prevalent topic over time). Table 1 displays, in no particular order, the resulting topic labels.

In keeping with the CTE's purpose, several topics (such as eco-labels or fishing subsidies) blend environmental and economic considerations. But some (such as precautionary principle or Doha sustainability/mandate) tilt more toward environmental, and others (such as market access or intellectual property) tilt more toward economic. A final set of topics (such as inter-institutional coordination or proposals to change WTO rules) are administrative.

Together these topics comprise the overall corpus, and a single paragraph can host more than one topic. Indeed, recall that the computer does what humans alone cannot: for each unit of analysis, it calculates how many topics appear and in what proportions. Here, the topic with the highest proportion (over 8%) is an administrative topic: papers that governments circulate to spur discussion. Then, two topics with greater substance-market access and sustainable developmentaccount for nearly 5% each. Seven additional topics (trade restrictions, fishing subsidies, trade/environment tradeoffs, meeting reports, eco-labels, intellectual property, research) account for nearly 4% each. Conversely, some topics (e.g. environmental services, sustainable forestry, precautionary principle)-account for less than 2% each.

The computer-identified topics are much richer than the CTE's 10-point mandate. Governments not only cover mandated items unequally, they also discuss single mandated items in several different ways. The computer uncovers these as distinct topics. For example, multilateral environmental agreements (MEAs) are just one mandated item, but the reading/labeling process finds they are central to three computer-identified topics: (a) Trade Restrictions within MEAs; (b) Proposals to Change MEA rules, and (c) Coordination between MEAs and the WTO. As discussed in more detail in Part VI.A of the Appendix, these three topics represent three ways governments talk about these environmental policy tools: (a) an administrative way, merely examining MEAs' trade-related provisions; (b) a challenging way, proposing that MEA rules be changed; and (c) a supportive way, urging WTO-MEA coordination. (Below, we probe how each way correlates with governments' development levels.)

In contrast, some actually-discussed topics are not in the mandate. Both climate change and private standard-setting are examples of governments using the CTE to discuss non-mandated issues that, between 1995-2012, were gaining traction in international politics. Meanwhile, the topics of technology transfer and proposals to change WTO rules show governments being downright revisionist.

paragraphs: Now we turn to our two research questions. In a forum that is open to countries at all development levels and addresses the overlap of environmental and economic policy, are the topics discussed by richer states more environmental than those discussed by poorer ones? Also, as Brazil's, South Africa's, India's, and China's development levels have increased (or decreased), have they talked in a more (or less) environmental way?

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
sections.header_raw: Question 1: topics' association with development level
sections.header_norm: question 1 topics association with development level
paragraphs: To probe whether there is a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism, we regress speakers' topic proportions on their per capita GDP. Specifically, we use a Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO), a type of penalized regression: after initializing the parameters of a standard OLS at zero, its algorithm estimates the coefficients for every combination of parameters, biasing the results toward finding no substantive relationship. Coefficients nevertheless estimated as non-zero can be thought of as "significant," while others drop out. Results robust to this specification are less likely to be fragile to the specific combination of variables.

Figure 1 depicts the regression results for the key variable per capita GDP after controlling for year, country, paragraph number, polity scores. 17 Of the 32 topics, six immediately drop out as uncorrelated with development level: Agricultural Subsidies, Discussion Papers, Disputes, Inter-Institutional Conflict, Precautionary Principle, Proposals to Change MEA Rules. Then the remaining 26 topics are ordered by their coefficients, with the dotted line denoting zero. Topics to the line's left have negative coefficients; topics to the right have positive coefficients. The further a topic is from the line in either direction, the greater its coefficient's absolute value.

The LASSO regression is an extension of OLS, and the coefficients have an intuitive meaning. Topics on the far left are strongly associated with poorer countries. Topics on the far right are strongly associated with richer countries. 18 For some topics-such as Intellectual Property, Technology Transfer, and Fishing Subsidies-the correlation with per capita GDP is significant, but not extreme.

However, starkly "poor-country topics" include Market Access, Inter-Institutional Coordination, Proposals to Change WTO Rules, and Sectoral Analyses. Meanwhile, starkly "rich-country topics" include Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency, Climate Change, and Sustainable Forestry. Immediately, the rich-country topics appear more explicitly "environmental." Delving into the underlying texts reinforces that impression, as discussed in more detail in Part VI.B of the Appendix.

Cynically, one might wonder whether the proportion of statements that richer countries dedicate to environmental topics is merely a strategy for an economic goal: protecting their producers from competition. Although that simplistic explanation may be at play occasionally, overall it does not withstand close-reading of the texts. For instance, richer countries often raise topics (e.g. offering technology transfers to poorer countries) that would undercut such a goal. Moreover, statements by richer countries indicate that they raise environmental topics due to their own citizens' demands for greater environmental stewardship-and that conforms with the expectation that richer countries are more likely to have a robust middle class with strong concerns about non-economic issues. 19 To check whether our approach of "unsupervised" topic modeling truly adds value beyond what a human expert might intuit, we also did something novel. After assigning topic labels, we invited a small sample of scholars and practitioners with knowledge of environment/economic politics to take an anonymous survey, predicting whether each of the 32 computer-identified topics would be negatively, positively, or not correlated with countries' per capita GDP. Our own expectation, that environmental protection would be prioritized more by richer countries than by poorer countries, was not divulged. Of 20 invitees, 15 completed the entire survey. With its modest sample size, the survey is not fully scientific-but it does help in checking whether computerassisted techniques deliver something that humans alone do not.

The experts' predictions of the regression results hinted that the scholars and practitioners-without any knowledge of our expectation that richer countries' main topics in the CTE would tilt toward environmental, while poorer countries' main topics would tilt toward economic-held a similar intuition. Of the four topics that were most closely associated with richer countries, the experts correctly predicted two: Energy Efficiency and Sustainable Forestry. Of the four topics that were most closely associated with poorer countries, they correctly predicted one: Market Access. (For more details, see Part IV.E of the Appendix.)

Yet in addition to offering some preliminary validation for our conceptual framework, comparing the experts' predictions versus the actual regression results demonstrates the appropriateness of tackling our research question with a machine-learning approach that is "unsupervised" (Loughran & McDonald 2011). If-instead of ceding initial pattern recognition to the computer and letting the textual data speak for themselves-we had opted for a supervised approach in which the computer merely coded the topics that we or other experts thought would be relevant, we would have imposed tunnel vision on ourselves. By using an unsupervised approach in which human effort focuses on interpreting what the computer unearths, we discover topics and relationships that humans alone might miss, not have time to process, or not think to check (Bagozzi et al., 2019).

Apart from the expert exercise, both computerized distant-reading of the aggregated corpus and human close-reading of individual texts point in a similar direction: a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism. In this forum that addresses the overlap of environmental and economic policy, the topics that richer countries raise most have an environmental tone: Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency, Climate Change, Sustainable Forestry. But the topics that poorer countries raise most are starkly economic (like Market Access) or administrative (like Proposals to Change WTO Rules, Inter-Institutional Coordination, or Sectoral Analyses).

Such divisions are also apparent in a particular mandated item-MEAs-that is talked about in several different ways. Recall that, even though analyzing sentiment is not our purpose, the computer noted the different words used in various discussions and flagged these as distinct topics. One topic urges WTO-MEA coordination and is clearly supportive of MEAs, but another topic neutrally examines MEAs' trade-related provisions and is mostly administrative. When the regression calculates the correlation between topic proportions and per capita GDP, the supportive way is associated with richer countries and the administrative way is associated with poorer countries. This illustrates how governments for whom environmental protection is not the priority can deflect conversations about environmental protection without explicitly arguing against it.

Question 2: the BASIC countries' increased or decreased emphasis on particular topics Thus far the results show striking differences between richer and poorer countries, but what about Brazil, South Africa, India, and China in particular? With their dynamic and middling development levels, the BASIC countries are not identical to the poor South, or the rich North, or even to one another. These four countries are frequently lumped together, since they coordinate their positions in international environmental negotiations and, along with Russia, operate their own development bank. 20 Yet they differ economically, as shown in Figure A9 in the Appendix: India has by far the lowest per capita GDP, China has had the highest economic growth rates recently, and Brazil and South Africa actually experienced some declines in per capita GDP during the 1995-2012 period. 21 We capitalize on this variation, comparing the BASIC states to each other-and also, at different development levels, to themselves.

The regression results above found a significant relationship between topic proportions and per capita GDP for 26 topics. Space constraints do not permit analyses for all of them, so instead we focus on two: (1) Sustainable Development, an environmental topic strongly associated with richer countries, and (2) Market Access, an economic topic strongly associated with poorer countries. To probe these, we use the same basic regression framework and controls described above, except now we consider the 1995-2012 per capita GDP of only Brazil, South Africa, India, and China. 22 The question is whether they talk in a more "environmental" way-less about market access, more about sustainable development-when they are at higher development levels.

Panels (a)-(d) of Figure 2 show results for Sustainable Development, with confidence bands as dotted lines. When these four countries have been at higher levels of per capita GDP, have they discussed this topic more? 23 The results are intriguing. Across the 1995-2012 period, regardless of development level, both South Africa and China devoted about 4 percent of their statements to sustainable development. 24 Meanwhile, India never devoted more than 3 percent, and at higher per capita GDP, India actually talked about sustainable development slightly less. Brazil is the sole BASIC country for which higher per capita GDP is associated with talking about this topic more.

Notably, Brazil shifts in that direction above US $8,000-which, in this period, none of the other three countries have surpassed. This supports the notion that environmental protection is something that few countries seriously entertain until they surpass some development threshold (e.g. Cole et al., 1997;Grossman & Krueger, 1991;Hochstetler & Viola, 2012;Najam, 2005;Stern, 2017). Yet the threshold may be well below conventional definitions of a "highly developed country," 25 meaning a shift to greater environmentalism in international forums occurs even while still considered a developing country.

Next, panels (a)-(d) of Figure 3 show the results for Market Access. Above, the market access topic was strongly associated with poorer countries. So, at higher levels of per capita GDP, do the BASIC countries talk about this topic less?

Again, the results show the added value of examining these four important countries individually-after all, what governments emphasize in international forums could vary not only across countries but also within countries, as their development level changes over time. Like other developing countries, all of the BASIC countries do seem to care about market access: none devoted less than 4 percent of their statements to this topic, and some have devoted up to 8 percent. Yet the topic proportions exhibit intriguing correlations with per capita GDP. For South Africa, the proportions are steady. For India and China, the proportions dip slightly with increases at relatively low levels of per capita GDP, then do not decline further. But Brazil displays a straightforward negative correlation spanning various levels of per capita GDP.

As examined in further detail in Part VI.C of the Appendix, Brazil again fits the pattern of richer countries in the previous analyses of the full CTE membership. The three others fit the pattern of poorer countries. Again, an important factor may be that Brazil has a significantly higher development level than the other BASIC countries. Therefore, unlike the others, it is nearer a "tipping point" of being less fixated on economic issues and increasingly attuned to environmental protection.

Although our textual data from the CTE end in 2012, anecdotal evidence from the BASIC countries in more recent years reinforces the idea of a tipping point, potentially around per capita GDP of US $8,000. In the 2012-2021 period, China made increasingly aggressive moves to combat domestic pollution and global climate change 26 -but Brazil was less proactive, and even exhibited slippage in areas such as sustainable forestry. 27 A look at development levels in that period reveals patterns in line with the more detailed analyses above: China's per capita GDP surpassed US $8,000 around 2015 and has continued to increase, whereas Brazil's per capita GDP dropped markedly around 2014 and has hovered near US $8,000 since then. 28 While this potential threshold needs to be tested further in future research, there is suggestive evidence that changes in development level are correlated with changes in rising powers' embrace of environmental protection in recent years as well.

Next, panels (a)-(d) of Figure 3 show the results for Market Access. Above, the market access topic was strongly associated with poorer countries. So, at higher levels of per capita GDP, do the BASIC countries talk about this topic less?

Again, the results show the added value of examining these four important countries individually-after all, what governments emphasize in international forums could vary not only across countries but also within countries, as their development level changes over time. Like other developing countries, all of the BASIC countries do seem to care about market access: none devoted less than 4 percent of their statements to this topic, and some have devoted up to 8 percent. Yet the topic proportions exhibit intriguing correlations with per capita GDP. For South Africa, the proportions are steady. For India and China, the proportions dip slightly with increases at relatively low levels of per capita GDP, then do not decline further. But Brazil displays a straightforward negative correlation spanning various levels of per capita GDP.

As examined in further detail in Part VI.C of the Appendix, Brazil again fits the pattern of richer countries in the previous analyses of the full CTE membership. The three others fit the pattern of poorer countries. Again, an important factor may be that Brazil has a significantly higher development level than the other BASIC countries. Therefore, unlike the others, it is nearer a "tipping point" of being less fixated on economic issues and increasingly attuned to environmental protection.

Although our textual data from the CTE end in 2012, anecdotal evidence from the BASIC countries in more recent years reinforces the idea of a tipping point, potentially around per capita GDP of US $8,000. In the 2012-2021 period, China made increasingly aggressive moves to combat domestic pollution and global climate change 26 -but Brazil was less proactive, and even exhibited slippage in areas such as sustainable forestry. 27 A look at development levels in that period reveals patterns in line with the more detailed analyses above: China's per capita GDP surpassed US $8,000 around 2015 and has continued to increase, whereas Brazil's per capita GDP dropped markedly around 2014 and has hovered near US $8,000 since then. 28 While this potential threshold needs to be tested further in future research, there is suggestive evidence that changes in development level are correlated with changes in rising powers' embrace of environmental protection in recent years as well.

To probe whether there is a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism, we regress speakers' topic proportions on their per capita GDP. Specifically, we use a Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO), a type of penalized regression: after initializing the parameters of a standard OLS at zero, its algorithm estimates the coefficients for every combination of parameters, biasing the results toward finding no substantive relationship. Coefficients nevertheless estimated as non-zero can be thought of as "significant," while others drop out. Results robust to this specification are less likely to be fragile to the specific combination of variables.

Figure 1 depicts the regression results for the key variable per capita GDP after controlling for year, country, paragraph number, polity scores. 17 Of the 32 topics, six immediately drop out as uncorrelated with development level: Agricultural Subsidies, Discussion Papers, Disputes, Inter-Institutional Conflict, Precautionary Principle, Proposals to Change MEA Rules. Then the remaining 26 topics are ordered by their coefficients, with the dotted line denoting zero. Topics to the line's left have negative coefficients; topics to the right have positive coefficients. The further a topic is from the line in either direction, the greater its coefficient's absolute value.

The LASSO regression is an extension of OLS, and the coefficients have an intuitive meaning. Topics on the far left are strongly associated with poorer countries. Topics on the far right are strongly associated with richer countries. 18 For some topics-such as Intellectual Property, Technology Transfer, and Fishing Subsidies-the correlation with per capita GDP is significant, but not extreme.

However, starkly "poor-country topics" include Market Access, Inter-Institutional Coordination, Proposals to Change WTO Rules, and Sectoral Analyses. Meanwhile, starkly "rich-country topics" include Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency, Climate Change, and Sustainable Forestry. Immediately, the rich-country topics appear more explicitly "environmental." Delving into the underlying texts reinforces that impression, as discussed in more detail in Part VI.B of the Appendix.

Cynically, one might wonder whether the proportion of statements that richer countries dedicate to environmental topics is merely a strategy for an economic goal: protecting their producers from competition. Although that simplistic explanation may be at play occasionally, overall it does not withstand close-reading of the texts. For instance, richer countries often raise topics (e.g. offering technology transfers to poorer countries) that would undercut such a goal. Moreover, statements by richer countries indicate that they raise environmental topics due to their own citizens' demands for greater environmental stewardship-and that conforms with the expectation that richer countries are more likely to have a robust middle class with strong concerns about non-economic issues. 19 To check whether our approach of "unsupervised" topic modeling truly adds value beyond what a human expert might intuit, we also did something novel. After assigning topic labels, we invited a small sample of scholars and practitioners with knowledge of environment/economic politics to take an anonymous survey, predicting whether each of the 32 computer-identified topics would be negatively, positively, or not correlated with countries' per capita GDP. Our own expectation, that environmental protection would be prioritized more by richer countries than by poorer countries, was not divulged. Of 20 invitees, 15 completed the entire survey. With its modest sample size, the survey is not fully scientific-but it does help in checking whether computerassisted techniques deliver something that humans alone do not.

The experts' predictions of the regression results hinted that the scholars and practitioners-without any knowledge of our expectation that richer countries' main topics in the CTE would tilt toward environmental, while poorer countries' main topics would tilt toward economic-held a similar intuition. Of the four topics that were most closely associated with richer countries, the experts correctly predicted two: Energy Efficiency and Sustainable Forestry. Of the four topics that were most closely associated with poorer countries, they correctly predicted one: Market Access. (For more details, see Part IV.E of the Appendix.)

Yet in addition to offering some preliminary validation for our conceptual framework, comparing the experts' predictions versus the actual regression results demonstrates the appropriateness of tackling our research question with a machine-learning approach that is "unsupervised" (Loughran & McDonald 2011). If-instead of ceding initial pattern recognition to the computer and letting the textual data speak for themselves-we had opted for a supervised approach in which the computer merely coded the topics that we or other experts thought would be relevant, we would have imposed tunnel vision on ourselves. By using an unsupervised approach in which human effort focuses on interpreting what the computer unearths, we discover topics and relationships that humans alone might miss, not have time to process, or not think to check (Bagozzi et al., 2019).

Apart from the expert exercise, both computerized distant-reading of the aggregated corpus and human close-reading of individual texts point in a similar direction: a positive correlation between development level and environmentalism. In this forum that addresses the overlap of environmental and economic policy, the topics that richer countries raise most have an environmental tone: Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency, Climate Change, Sustainable Forestry. But the topics that poorer countries raise most are starkly economic (like Market Access) or administrative (like Proposals to Change WTO Rules, Inter-Institutional Coordination, or Sectoral Analyses).

Such divisions are also apparent in a particular mandated item-MEAs-that is talked about in several different ways. Recall that, even though analyzing sentiment is not our purpose, the computer noted the different words used in various discussions and flagged these as distinct topics. One topic urges WTO-MEA coordination and is clearly supportive of MEAs, but another topic neutrally examines MEAs' trade-related provisions and is mostly administrative. When the regression calculates the correlation between topic proportions and per capita GDP, the supportive way is associated with richer countries and the administrative way is associated with poorer countries. This illustrates how governments for whom environmental protection is not the priority can deflect conversations about environmental protection without explicitly arguing against it.

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
paragraphs: Using a natural language processing (NLP) approach, we asked and answered two key questions. First, when governments are free to talk about environmental protection alongside other issues, are the topics discussed by richer states more environmental than those discussed by poorer ones? Second, as Brazil, South Africa, India, and China's development levels have increased (or decreased), have they talked in a more (or less) environmental way? After controlling for several confounders, higher development levels are associated with conducting more "environmental" discussions. Within the CTE, governments are free to raise their environmental priorities, economic priorities, or combinations of the two. While few governments explicitly say that they do not prioritize the natural environment, the topics they emphasize are revealing. The topics that richer countries talk about most-Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency, Climate Change, and Sustainable Forestry-are explicitly environmental. That sharply contrasts with poorer countries, whose most prevalent topics are administrative (Proposals to Change WTO Rules, Inter-Institutional Coordination, or Sectoral Analyses) or starkly economic (Market Access). This divide is also apparent in close-reading of the underlying texts and in analyses of how various governments talk about MEAs.

Focusing on Brazil, South Africa, India, and China, we then capitalize on how development levels vary across and within the BASIC countries. The analyses indicate that Brazil has talked in a more environmental way-less about market access, more about sustainable development-when it has been at higher development levels. Meanwhile South Africa, India, and China have talked in a less environmental way, and part of the reason may be because their development levels lagged behind Brazil's. Since 2012, preliminary evidence suggests that Brazil's environmentalism has waned (as its development level also has waned), while China's environmentalism has increased (as its development level also has increased).

sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
paragraphs: Several factors made the WTO's CTE a compelling empirical context for our research questions. With a nearly global membership and a system of recurrent meetings over the years, the CTE delivers longitudinal textual data about many countries at very different development levels. Moreover, while the CTE covers more than strictly environmental forums, its medium-sized mandate makes the potential topics more bounded than in a catch-all forum such as the UN General Assembly. The CTE's one-country-one-vote system is common to many international forums and incentivizes a wide variety of governments to share their views. Ultimate voting occurs in separate WTO meetings-the eventuality of voting makes the stakes high enough to discourage unfettered "cheap talk," and separating negotiations from voting reduces temptations for governments to deliver lengthy lies about their priorities in CTE meetings in order to manipulate voting outcomes. All of this results in rich data from negotiating transcripts that occur naturally: the CTE texts depict conversations in which poorer and richer governments can freely discuss their environmental priorities, economic priorities, or both.

But no study can do everything, and examining the WTO's CTE does present some limitations. For example, the World Trade Organization's membership is still a few dozen countries short of being truly universal. In addition, neither the WTO nor its CTE produces full-fledged "legislation" that could be analyzed as scholars do for bodies like the U.S. Congress. Furthermore, the CTE covers economic and environmental issues, but it does not extend to policy issues such as military/defense. Therefore, building on our foundation, future research should examine additional international forums and probe two areas of generalizability. First, would the finding that countries at higher development levels talk in a more "environmental" way hold in any international forum, from the very narrow (e.g. the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change) to the very broad (e.g. the UN General Assembly)? And second, is the evidence from Brazil more generally true, with various governments talking like rich countries when they are past a particular per capita GDP threshold, such as US $8,000?

Concerning other forums: we believe our findings from the Committee on Trade and Environment can travel-particularly if the forum is not about environmental protection alone (Johnson, 2016). Forums such as the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change are strictly environmental, so governments encounter greater pressure to pay lip service to environmental protection and face fewer openings to shift discussions to non-environmental priorities. Our results are more likely to hold in a broader forum like the UN General Assembly, where environmental issues can be discussed alongside others.

Concerning thresholds: US $8,000 per capita GDP is a testable conjecture derived from how Brazil differs from the other BASIC countries and from its own behavior during economic downturns. Outside of the period of analysis, anecdotal evidence involving China also supports the notion of such a threshold and suggests that governments might talk less like developing countries even before becoming a bona fide rich country. Future research-perhaps examining additional countries hovering near this potential tipping point-would be needed to pinpoint exactly where any threshold exists.
