sections.paper_id: Johnson and Lerner - 2023 - Environmentalism among poor and rich countries using natural language processing to handle perfunct
paragraphs: This article makes substantive contributions to international political economy debates about the environment/development nexus (Barkin, 2003;Franzen & Vogl, 2013a;Inglehart, 2008;Jinnah, 2017;Johnson & Urpelainen, 2020;Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012;Mitchell, 2010;Najam, 2005;Neumayer, 2001;Stern, 2017). We find that, overall, environmentalism is a lesser priority among poorer countries; however, there also is evidence that countries become more "environmental" even at development levels well below traditional thresholds for being considered a high-income economy. Such findings contribute to the literature on rising powers (e.g. Donno & Rudra, 2014;Hallding et al., 2013;Hopewell, 2015;Weinhardt & ten Brink, 2020) by suggesting that the BASIC countries are more likely to prioritize environmental considerations over economic ones once they surmount some tipping point related to meeting their basic needs.

The findings also connect and refine two seemingly opposed literatures: the Environmental Kuznets Curve (e.g. Carson, 2010;Cole et al., 1997;Grossman & Krueger, 1991;Selden & Song, 1994;Stern, 2017) and the Environmentalism of the Poor (e.g. Davey, 2009;Guha, 2000;Martinez-Alier, 2014;Retallack, 2000). As previously detailed, our outcome of interest and numerous other features diverge from the EKC literature-nevertheless, our findings do suggest a positive relationship between development and environmentalism, as well as the existence of some sort of tipping point. At the same time, our analysis also lends support to EOP proponents, who emphasize that countries do not need to be fully "rich" in order to be motivated to protect the natural environment. Rather than being diametrically opposed, the two literatures can be fruitfully connected and refined: the EKC literature must not presume that environmental action and sacrifice occur only in richer countries, and the EOP literature must not presume that poorer countries' environmental action and sacrifice is already widespread and consistent enough to produce a negative association between environmentalism and development level.

Our finding of a positive correlation between environmentalism and development level also expands on a warning from empirical work on international public opinion polls. Poorer individuals often express perfunctory support for environmentalism that they do not truly embrace, and once this is accounted for a clear "wealth effect" emerges (Franzen & Vogl, 2013a). Here, we find something analogous among poorer governments (though this is not to say that these governments cannot or will not prioritize the environment more in the future, as circumstances change).

In addition to these substantive contributions, we make methodological ones. First, our project warns about the dangers of investigating the environment/development relationship only in overwhelmingly environmental forums. Many researchers who are interested in governments' commitment to environmentalism look at explicitly environmental institutions, such as the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. That is valuable-but in such forums, governments also encounter greater pressure to pay lip service to environmental protection and face fewer openings to shift discussions to non-environmental priorities. Therefore, it is crucial to scrutinize institutions in which governments consider multiple policy areas. When other goals are at stake, governments who do not prioritize environmental protection may deflect conversations about the environment by raising administrative points, calling for more research, or shifting the conversation to another policy area.

Second, our work demonstrates how formal mandates can be incomplete, even misleading, for categorizing discussions within political institutions. Had we taken the CTE's mandate literally, as the basis of a codebook list of which topics could arise, much of the reality would have been missed. Instead, by permitting the textual data to speak for themselves, we uncover how governments dodge some parts of the mandate, discuss a single mandated item in several different ways, and raise additional topics that were not mandated at all. Since formal rules struggle to prevent government officials from raising whichever topics they wish, scholars should suspect that institutional mandates are often poor guides to what is actually discussed in domestic or international institutions.

We note how this approach blends positivism and interpretivism, social science and computer science, quantitative and qualitative data. We link with work in comparative and American politics (e.g. Catalinac, 2016;Grimmer, 2010;Monroe et al., 2008;Nielsen, 2017;Terman, 2017) to extend best practices and demonstrate how conscientious computer-assisted textual analysis can process the massive amounts of textual data generated in the international relations subfield (Bagozzi & Berliner, 2017;Tingley, 2017). This article makes substantive contributions to international political economy debates about the environment/development nexus (Barkin, 2003;Franzen & Vogl, 2013a;Inglehart, 2008;Jinnah, 2017;Johnson & Urpelainen, 2020;Michaelowa & Michaelowa, 2012;Mitchell, 2010;Najam, 2005;Neumayer, 2001;Stern, 2017). We find that, overall, environmentalism is a lesser priority among poorer countries; however, there also is evidence that countries become more "environmental" even at development levels well below traditional thresholds for being considered a high-income economy. Such findings contribute to the literature on rising powers (e.g. Donno & Rudra, 2014;Hallding et al., 2013;Hopewell, 2015;Weinhardt & ten Brink, 2020) by suggesting that the BASIC countries are more likely to prioritize environmental considerations over economic ones once they surmount some tipping point related to meeting their basic needs.

The findings also connect and refine two seemingly opposed literatures: the Environmental Kuznets Curve (e.g. Carson, 2010;Cole et al., 1997;Grossman & Krueger, 1991;Selden & Song, 1994;Stern, 2017) and the Environmentalism of the Poor (e.g. Davey, 2009;Guha, 2000;Martinez-Alier, 2014;Retallack, 2000). As previously detailed, our outcome of interest and numerous other features diverge from the EKC literature-nevertheless, our findings do suggest a positive relationship between development and environmentalism, as well as the existence of some sort of tipping point. At the same time, our analysis also lends support to EOP proponents, who emphasize that countries do not need to be fully "rich" in order to be motivated to protect the natural environment. Rather than being diametrically opposed, the two literatures can be fruitfully connected and refined: the EKC literature must not presume that environmental action and sacrifice occur only in richer countries, and the EOP literature must not presume that poorer countries' environmental action and sacrifice is already widespread and consistent enough to produce a negative association between environmentalism and development level.

Our finding of a positive correlation between environmentalism and development level also expands on a warning from empirical work on international public opinion polls. Poorer individuals often express perfunctory support for environmentalism that they do not truly embrace, and once this is accounted for a clear "wealth effect" emerges (Franzen & Vogl, 2013a).
